What it is: Severity of decompression relative to what the fish was acclimated to — the dominant barotrauma dose metric.
How it is calculated: Acclimation (exposure-onset) absolute pressure divided by nadir absolute pressure: RPC = P_acclim / P_nadir. NB: some authors invert it (R(NE:A) = P_nadir / P_acclim, a value <1).
Used by (examples): Brown 2012; Carlson 2012; Boys 2016; Pflugrath 2018; Kerr 2023; Zitek 2026
Key pitfalls / reproducibility issues: DIRECTION IS INCONSISTENT across papers (acclim/nadir >1 vs nadir/acclim <1) — a major comparability problem. Meaningless without the acclimation assumption.

What it is: Log transform of the pressure ratio used in logistic mortal-injury models.
How it is calculated: Natural log of the pressure ratio: LRP = ln(P_acclim / P_nadir).
Key pitfalls / reproducibility issues: Same acclimation dependence as RPC; log base (ln vs log10) not always stated.

What it is: Lowest absolute pressure experienced during exposure/passage.
How it is calculated: Measured minimum of the pressure-time trace (chamber) or modeled/Sensor-Fish minimum (turbine).
Key pitfalls / reproducibility issues: Absolute vs gauge pressure sometimes ambiguous; field nadir is modeled/estimated, not measured on the fish.

What it is: Pressure (or depth) the fish equilibrated its swim bladder to; the baseline for RPC/LRP.
How it is calculated: Set by holding depth, or measured via neutral-buoyancy / swim-bladder-volume tests (e.g. excess-mass or rupture test).
Key pitfalls / reproducibility issues: The single biggest assumption in barotrauma work; wild fish may not be neutrally buoyant at the intake. Acclimation duration also varies (often <24 h).

What it is: Binary 'mortally injured' classification replacing raw mortality.
How it is calculated: Fish classified mortally injured if ANY of a defined set of injuries (8 in McKinstry 2007) is present.
Key pitfalls / reproducibility issues: The injury set and severity thresholds differ between labs — not standardized.

What it is: Probability of a specific injury (swim-bladder rupture, exophthalmia, emboli, etc.) as a function of dose.
How it is calculated: Logistic regression of injury presence vs RPC/LRP (or nadir).
Key pitfalls / reproducibility issues: Injury taxonomies and which injuries count as 'mortal' are inconsistent; model forms differ.

What it is: Counts or graded severity of injury types per fish.
How it is calculated: Sum/grade of observed injuries from necropsy / X-ray / transparent-fish observation.
Key pitfalls / reproducibility issues: No common injury catalogue or severity scale across studies — a core reproducibility gap.

What it is: Speed of the pressure drop to nadir — emphasized by dynamic (non-Boyle) barotrauma models.
How it is calculated: Pressure change per unit time (slope of pressure-time trace), or time-to-nadir.
Key pitfalls / reproducibility issues: Rarely reported; most chamber studies report only start/nadir, not the rate, despite its mechanistic importance.

What it is: Related gas-bubble-trauma metric (distinct from decompression barotrauma).
Key pitfalls / reproducibility issues: Different mechanism; should not be merged with decompression barotrauma but sometimes is.
